text.title: Resource Management Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules grounding the resource-management methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Plan to 75-80% effective capacity, never 100% — meetings, interruptions, admin overhead consume at least 20% of nominal hours.

rationale.source: DeMarco & Lister Peopleware (slack chapter); Float 70% rule

rule.rationale: Plans at 100% utilization collapse on the first sick week or sprint ceremony load.

rule.statement: Cross-train at least one backup per critical skill; bus factor below 2 is a P1 risk in the risk register.
rule.rationale: Single-person dependency on a critical skill is a project risk comparable to a high-probability technical risk.

rule.statement: Track skills with three levels (learning, proficient, mentor) AND a last-used date.
rule.rationale: People grow and skills change; matrices without dates produce mismatched assignments within 6-12 months.

rule.statement: Resource plan must reconcile with schedule (CPM) and budget (EVM); update all three together when resource changes occur.
rule.rationale: A resource change that does not propagate creates invisible drift — the plan says on-time but the critical path has slipped.

rule.statement: Keep a stretch bench — people with 10-20% slack who can absorb spikes without going over 80% effective capacity.
rule.rationale: Without a stretch bench, every unplanned event requires a formal escalation.

rule.statement: Redact calendar subject lines before any LLM input; keep only duration and recurrence pattern.
rule.rationale: Calendar subjects are often sensitive (layoff prep, performance reviews) and may not flow to a model.

list.item: Stable single team under 8 people — informal allocation works
Pure agile teams committed to whole-team ownership — utilization tracking erodes safety
Organisations where utilization % is a performance metric — creates burnout and gaming
